raw_payload.author: Allnoir_ovthings
raw_payload.content:
It's well acted, well paced, and it captures the enormity of the trials. But as I watched I couldn't help feeling like it only brushed the surface of something far more complex, which is the psychology of the men being judged.

The film touches on it here and there with glimpses of pride, denial, rationalization, but never quite lets us sit in their mindset long enough to understand how such thinking takes root. I don't mean sympathy, but understanding.

Most films about this era stay safely on the side of "righteousness". A movie that truly follows the "bad" side, that dares to dissect the Nazi perspective without flinching could make one of the most unsettling and important films ever made. Nuremberg is strong, but it leaves that untold story still waiting for someone brave enough to tell it.

Maybe the reason the psychiatrist ends up committing 13 years later is because he was having a moral debate with himself over the trials and the "good" and "bad".

raw_payload.author: ariazarei2004
raw_payload.content: I had so many expectations for this movie, but unfortunately it couldn't fulfill them all. I can say that this film has actually distorted history in some places and has not shown the events as they were. If you have watched the film carefully and are someone who has read history, or even if you have not read history but have a curious mind, you will come across these flaws with a simple investigation. First of all, I really admire Russell Crowe's performance in this film. One of the most fundamental things that was not shown in this film was Hermann Göring's weight loss. In reality, Göring had become so thin that he looked small in his clothes, and this historical fact was not shown at all in the film. Another fundamental and important fact was that Hermann Göring never used the words "Long Live Hitler" in court, and none of the surviving documents from the Nuremberg Trials show this, while in the film we see her uttering such a statement in front of everyone in court. Another issue that could have been shown in the film but wasn't was Hermann Göring's mockery of the trial, which actually happened. In court, Göring sometimes behaved as if he were the judge and actually sometimes beat the prosecutor, and this was not shown in the film. One of the other most important things that was not shown in the film but happened in reality was Göring giving the military salute at the first trial. (A normal military salute with a right hand next to the temple, not the Nazi military salute.) There are many such facts, none of which were shown in the film, and the main focus was more on the psychologist than on Göring himself and the things he did in court. We immediately see that his court session is shown very little in the film and only occurs in two parts. Well, for a film called Nuremberg and reminiscent of the Nuremberg Trials, this was a very wrong and weak act. It could have been much better and stronger, but unfortunately it didn't meet all my expectations, but overall it was an entertaining film and Hermann Göring was played beautifully by the actor.

raw_payload.author: PerryAtTheMovies
raw_payload.content:
7.7/10 (Recommend)

First reaction is that this movie deserves to be commended on more than saying the acting was good, the story was heavy, and the pacing was decent. All three of these things ring true, but it was also more than that. It was thought provoking in its humanity, morality, and legality.

So here is my checklist of this film:


* The acting was good, but I don't think any of it was award winning. The variety of actors chosen for each role made everything feel genuine for the era. Most impressive was Russell Crowe actually speaking German. I assumed it would be him speaking English the entire time, but with a German accent. Beyond the acting feeling authentic the sets felt real too. There wasn't just a ton of close-up shots trying to convince you that you were in war torn Germany. They gave you brief avenues to truly see.



* The story was heavy. You know why they're in Nuremberg, and you know the atrocities that took place. But you know when you don't hear a single crunching of popcorn or rustling of snacks, and there's a collective holding of breath, then you know the director has reeled you in. (Humanity) Emotionally it was more pain and hurt seeing the atrocities and not just tear jerking.



* The pacing was good. Despite its 2.5 hour runtime, the pacing was enjoyable. There's very few moments of down time. I would recommend not going to see the film if you've either been sitting all day, or are tired, or both. I was restless throughout because of both of those things.



* The legality and morality of Nuremberg was not something I ever really considered before. These trials were unprecedented and so the cases had to be built from the ground up. It only shows a partiality of this, but it goes just deep enough to give you a sense.


Overall, this is a movie I'd recommend seeing in theatres. The sound quality is spectacular and the visuals are great on the big screen. This isn't an award winning film by any stretch of the imagination, but it does its job well enough to say "these were some of the first men to be tried in Nuremberg. We try to humanize them, but in the end they are evil." It might be long for some, but for others it's good entertainment and possibly the beginning of a knowledge quest.

That's it for now. Thanks for taking time to read my review. Happy watching and enjoy the show!

raw_payload.author: jfortier-24641
raw_payload.content: Wow, saw this at TIFF. Powerful film. Russell Crowe, Rami Malick, Michael Shannon were all fantastic , as well as the rest of the cast. Harrowing scenes of the Final Solution. You could hear a pin drop in the theater when they were showing actual scenes of Nazi atrocities. Highly recommend watching this film!

raw_payload.author: gdgassiott
raw_payload.content: Do not listen to the want to be Historians on this site. Was the movie 100% accurate, absolutely not, but neither was Band of Brothers, The Pacific, Fury, etc. However, this movie was very well done and told another side of the Nuremberg trials. My wife is not someone who watches WW2 movies, but she was captivated by this movie. Russel Crowe did a wonderful job playing Goering. Do yourself a favor and go watch the movie and enjoy it. It really is a movie that will stick with you for awhile.

raw_payload.author: dchdrz
raw_payload.content: Despite its impressive cast, "Nuremberg" never rises above the level of a standard blockbuster. Russell Crowe delivers a charismatic performance as Hermann Göring, capturing both his charm and menace. Rami Malek, as the American psychologist, is equally convincing, if sometimes deliberately irritating. But strong acting alone can't save the film from its shallow storytelling and predictable structure. The final act slips into a clichéd hero narrative, where the psychologist suddenly redeems himself in a scene that feels more tiresome than triumphant. Eventually, the film's attempt at a pointe isn't misguided, but it feels forced.

raw_payload.author: benlenzen
raw_payload.content:
This film was incredibly good. The soundtrack was spectacular, in the cinema you could just FEEL the historic weight of the events the film centers around. The pacing was also perfect, for a historic drama, its 140 minutes felt incredibly short. The cast and the acting was great as well. But most importantly, the deeper meaning and the messages of the film are just so important, and were delivered spectacularly. You just walk out with a pit in your stomach.

I still had some minor problems with the film, for example that it has some of the classic Hollywood US-centrism. I appreciate that the film was based mainly on a book, focusing on the relationship between the american psychiatrist and Göring, but I would have loved some more focus on other important figures. We didn't hear one single word from a russian or french judge or prosecutor for example. Some moments also felt a bit too unrealistic or theatrical for me, Jacksons meeting with the pope comes to mind, or the role and treatment of the psychiatrist in some parts. Also, it would have been great if Görings actor could have been german, but Russell Crowe makes up for it with an incredible performance, so I'll let that one go.

All in all the positives far outweigh the negatives for me though. A highly important, thought-provoking and entertaining film.

raw_payload.author: dweston-38669
raw_payload.content:
As a person who has , rather unhealthy, fallen down the rabbit hole of WW2 trials and atrocities, I never heard that nasty piece of work antisemitic Julius Streicher broke down and cried. It's just a fabrication this film adds to see this horrible,nasty person gets his just deserts. It was actually rather laughable.

I also could swallow Kelley had a ' connection' with Goring's wife. I appreciate he needed her to get more information on him but it felt tacked on.

Just as laughable is Rami Malek's performance as Douglas Kelley. He seems too mannered,too immature, doesn't convince as a psychiatrist. He also looks Jewish,which would have been a deterrent in the interviews he conducted with high level Nazis. The real Kelley didn't look too Jewish,maybe some dark features.

Russell Crowe steals the film as Goring. Pompous, arrogant and self delusional. His accent is flawless.

It has a rather bombastic soundtrack at times, which ,I guess, adds to the pomp of the film.

It's not bad and I would recommend especially to the new generation of younger people so they are aware of this important part of history and how intolerance can breed.

A misstep from James Vanderbilt,who wrote of the finest films of the last 25yr, Zodiac.

raw_payload.author: drael64
raw_payload.content:
The positive. It's very well acted, perfectly paced, has good cinematography. Overall it's a compelling peice of cinema. It does maintain tension.

However, it's not without substantive flaws. The dialogue is written to be too modern - slightly too informal for the period, even for informal speech, lacking plausible idiom and missing some period wording. What starts as potentially an interesting character study on Göring, as well as historically significant slice of time, as the run time moves on, collapses into familiar moral shorthand surrounding the Holocaust, and in doing so looses it's most interesting tension.

And that is understandable of course, but it's very explored territory, and doesn't offer any fresh understanding of the Nazi's, or the Allies. Indeed it romanticizes the egalitarianism of the Allies, in a way that doesn't accurately reflect the West, or the United States, in 1945. It, like so many others, mythologizes historical events with a modern culture lens, and plays it safe.

In summary, one might hope for more literary style realism at the start of this film, but it feels trapped by the same post-ww2 modern framework. It's still a compelling film, but short of it's potential.

raw_payload.author: gremlinlord615
raw_payload.content:
Saw an early screening of Nuremberg with the Regal Mystery Movie. Honestly, I was expecting another standard WWII film. There are two storylines that interweave. The first being the obvious, the relationship between Douglas Kelly (Malek) and Herman Goering (Crowe). And the story of trying to get the trials set up with Robert Jackson (Shannon).

Positives!

+Some of the most genuine conversations I have heard on film.

+Great set and costumes.

+Fitting Score.

+Interesting interpersonal relationships. Show what it means to put humanity before hatred.

Negatives!

-Some historical inaccuracies.

-Gimmicky black and white news reel moments.

-Rami Malek doesn't always match his words to his face.

Nuremberg is a great film. But not a documentary. Your enjoyment may vary because of that. But Russell Crowe knocks it out of the park for sure. No heavy handed themes. Just factual footage from the camps.

--GremlinLord615.

-Full Review on Youtube.

raw_payload.author: rjdedwards
raw_payload.content:
Of course this is an important topic and it's a story that needs to be told. If nothing else to help us better understand the motivations that can lead people to such evil.

A great cast has been assembled and there is a great movie to be made. But this isn't it. It's far from terrible but I came away thinking it was a bit meh as a piece of art. Take nothing away from the actors who did a fine job, especially the leads.

Maybe in the age of the streamers where stories are told in great depth over many episodes and seasons, it becomes tougher to present significant work in a 2hr frame.

For me it felt superficial, in the sense that there were countless opportunities to dig deeper into characters and relationships but, in the urge to cover ground, it felt like we skimmed the surface only.

It felt pedestrian. Obviously in fact-based stories the base facts are known so there can be little tension or jeopardy associated with storyline. But I felt we could have gone way deeper on the people, their personalities and their relationships. I felt there was a LOT more to explore between Goring and Kelly, the ebb and flow of ego, the motivation, the definition of success etc etc

So all in all a bit disappointing.

raw_payload.author: dlmiley
raw_payload.content: I saw this film at the AFI Film Fest in Hollywood. I was not aware of the psychiatrist's (Rami Malek) involvement with Goering (Russell Crowe) before and during the trial, and that was some of the best parts of the movie. Also interesting was the part that Sgt. Howie Triest (Leo Woodhall) played during the trial - I had not heard of his story before, and I have read and studied a lot about WWII. Russell Crowe is brilliant as Goering and Malek is very good but not perfect. Now for some of the issues I have the film. The portrayal of Goering on the witness stand was very good, but it was not accurate - the movie implies that it was all in one day and it was not. Also, I did not like Slattery's over the top performance as the warden of the prison. Michael Shannon's performance as Justice Jackson was adequate but not compelling or accurate. There are some other minor inaccuracies that cause me to delete a couple of points from an otherwise excellent film, but I definitely recommend it for fans of fine drama.

raw_payload.author: virindra
raw_payload.content:
Hermann Göring (Russell Crowe), Hitler's right-hand man, is imprisoned after the fall of Nazi Germany alongside other high-ranking leaders. Psychiatrist Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek) is tasked with assessing Göring's mental state and determining his role within the Nazi hierarchy. Meanwhile, Justice Robert H. Jackson (Michael Shannon) prepares for the prosecution. As Douglas becomes increasingly fascinated, even charmed, by Göring's intellect and manipulation, he begins to doubt whether Jackson can truly outmatch him in the courtroom.

Rami Malek, Michael Shannon, Richard E. Grant, and Colin Firth deliver committed performances. Malek has a few standout emotional moments that genuinely elevate the script. Shannon is characteristically sharp, though his presence is frustratingly limited. Richard E. Grant's Sir David Maxwell-Fyfe dominates the last scene, unintentionally overshadowing Shannon simply because his character is written with more bite.

As for Russell Crowe: he doesn't quite disappear into Göring. He's still Russell Crowe, the face of Gladiator, A Beautiful Mind, the familiar physical presence. The added weight may mirror Göring's later years, but it doesn't add psychological heft to his performance. Someone like Kevin Spacey chilling, calm, reptilian might have captured the unnerving charisma of Göring with more authenticity. Crowe feels more like a well-known actor performing a war criminal than inhabiting one.

The film is long and feels long. Several scenes, especially the extended philosophical conversations between Douglas and Howie, play like filler: moments that pad the runtime rather than deepen the narrative.

The story often circles around its ideas instead of advancing them, resulting in a movie that's about war crimes, guilt, accountability, and manipulation... yet somehow lacks urgency.

There are sharp moments. When Rami Malek hits emotional gear, you feel it. When Grant enters the courtroom, the dialogue finally snaps to life. But these highlights are too sparse to rescue the entire film. And with such a powerful cast, expectations were naturally high maybe too high for the uneven script to sustain.

Nuremberg brings together a heavyweight cast to dramatize the psychological chess match behind the post-WWII trials, but despite the pedigree, the film never reaches the emotional or historical gravity it aims for.

Nuremberg is respectable, watchable, and occasionally gripping, but never fully compelling. With this cast, the movie should have hit harder. Instead, it lands somewhere in the middle: thoughtful, but not profound; well-acted, but not transformative; long, but not deep.

raw_payload.author: SAMTHEBESTEST
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg (2025) : Movie Review -

Stanley Kramer made many good films in his career, but Judgment at Nuremberg easily surpasses them all and remains one of my most cherished films of the 1960s. While Hollywood at the time was basking in colorful romantic comedies and anti-war dramas, this black-and-white courtroom tale about the aftermath of Nazi atrocities made all those films look colorless. Reimagining or rediscovering this monumental story six decades later was never going to be easy, nor was it ever possible to match the cinematic legacy of Kramer's masterpiece. Yet, I appreciate that the filmmakers at least tried to explore something new - a psychological perspective rather than a legal one. The 2025 version of Nuremberg unfolds not in the courtroom but inside the mind of a psychiatrist. The film is told largely from the perspective of Dr. Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek), a chief psychiatrist assigned to assess the mental state of Nazi officials before their trials. This shift in focus leaves less room for the kind of gripping courtroom exchanges that defined the 1961 original - a decision that ultimately weakens the film's dramatic punch. However, despite its uneven storytelling, Nuremberg offers a few shocking and powerful moments that deserve acknowledgment.

The story begins ahead of the historic trials, as Dr. Kelley arrives at the prison to evaluate whether the Nazi war criminals are fit to stand trial. Among them is Hermann Göring played by Russell Crowe, in one of his most commanding late-career performances. Douglas, sharp and analytical, quickly finds himself drawn into a psychological chess match with Göring, who manipulates conversations with unsettling ease. What starts as a routine evaluation soon turns into a dangerous intellectual duel. The more Douglas studies Göring, the more he realizes that this man, despite being a monster, is incredibly intelligent - perhaps even one step ahead of him. Göring's every word, every smile, and every calculated silence reveal that he has meticulously planned his defense, right from his surrender to the courtroom proceedings. Douglas' challenge, then, is to find a way to outsmart him and ensure that justice prevails - ideally with the death penalty that Göring so richly deserves.

As mentioned earlier, this version of Nuremberg focuses more on the psychological warfare between doctor and prisoner than on the moral and legal debates that defined the original. The 1961 film remains one of the greatest courtroom dramas ever made, known for its striking cinematography, powerful dialogues, and moral gravity. In contrast, this new film seems visually flat and emotionally uneven. The courtroom scenes lack tension, the debates feel half-hearted, and the production doesn't even attempt to recreate the haunting grandeur of Kramer's vision.

Yet, one sequence stands out above all - the concentration camp footage. The moment those images appear on screen, the audience, much like the characters within the film, is left speechless. The horror, the inhumanity, and the raw documentation of suffering cut through the mediocrity of the rest of the film. That brief sequence alone carries the emotional weight that much of the film fails to sustain. Unfortunately, once that moment passes, the narrative returns to its middling rhythm. The confrontations between Hermann and Douglas, the prosecution's strategies, and the courtroom exchanges all lack the emotional depth and sense of urgency that the subject matter demands.

Still, the performances elevate the film considerably. Russell Crowe once again proves that he doesn't need elaborate direction or flashy writing to command attention. His portrayal of Göring is layered, frightening, and yet disturbingly charismatic. The German accent, the smug composure, and the subtle psychological manipulation - all are delivered with Crowe's signature intensity. Meanwhile, Rami Malek delivers a performance that often rivals, and occasionally surpasses, Crowe's. As Dr. Douglas Kelley, he portrays curiosity, moral confusion, and internal conflict with admirable restraint. I have admired Malek since Bohemian Rhapsody, and this role reinforces his range and maturity as an actor. The supporting cast - including Leo Woodall, John Slattery, Colin Hanks, Mark O'Brien, Wrenn Schmidt, Lydia Peckham, Michael Shannon, and Richard E. Grant - contributes solid performances that round out the ensemble.

Where the film falters most is in its technical execution. The cinematography is disappointingly ordinary - a serious letdown given the historical depth and emotional potential of the subject. The editing feels inconsistent and doesn't build tension effectively. The art direction, though passable, never immerses the viewer in post-war Germany. Even the background score feels muted, rarely enhancing the mood or atmosphere. The opening shot - a man urinating on a Nazi emblem - hints at a provocative tone, but that audacity vanishes soon after. Director James Vanderbilt keeps the story grounded in realism and authenticity, but his approach lacks cinematic fire. The pacing drags, and the tone remains too subdued for a story of such moral and historical magnitude. What's missing here is the courage to confront the darkness with full force. Kramer's Judgment at Nuremberg didn't just depict history - it made viewers feel its moral anguish. This new Nuremberg merely observes it. It's a psychological study that forgets to engage the heart. The film had the potential to be sharper, more brutal, and more introspective, but it settles for being "watchable" rather than unforgettable. And yet, for all its flaws, Nuremberg isn't a total misfire. It deserves credit for revisiting a crucial chapter of history and for attempting to interpret it through a fresh lens. Some scenes - especially those involving the camp footage and the verbal duels between Malek and Crowe - linger long after the credits roll. They remind us that beneath the film's surface lies the echo of something powerful, something that could have been extraordinary with a stronger vision.

In short, Nuremberg is a noble effort that falls short of greatness. It neither dishonors the original nor comes close to matching its brilliance. What it offers instead is a modern, psychologically inclined take on the same story - flawed but fascinating in moments. For history enthusiasts and fans of the actors involved, it's worth a viewing. But for those who have witnessed the timeless impact of the 1961 masterpiece, this new chapter will likely feel like an echo - faint, uneven, and yearning for the gravitas of its predecessor.

RATING - 5/10*

raw_payload.author: claykalinoski
raw_payload.content: Probably the best new movie I have seen in a very long time. Great performances and interesting plotlines. The "cat and mouse" relationship between Malek and Crowe is ever changing and complex. Very timeless story of the ascent of evil rings very true today. Much different than Judgment at Nuremberg, and maybe not as good, but still a must see movie.

raw_payload.author: woodswalker-11073
raw_payload.content: One of the best films I've seen in years, and I've seen many. Great performances across the board! I loved it! I simply couldn't stop watching! This one has awards written all over it. Malek, Crowe, and Shannon were amazing! I am so glad I went to the theater to see this film. People should not forget history.

raw_payload.author: KatyR-55
raw_payload.content: Nuremberg tells an important story, not very well, not in a memorable way, but not in a totally false way. It's a Hollywood blockbuster in style, fudging and over-simplifying the narrative as these blockbusters tends to do, and tending to overdramatise the US involvement. The film-makers here made decisions which cluttered the narrative with side stories (the pope's story was an invention, and the translator's story was a quite unnecessary distraction). The scriptwriters couldn't decide whether this was a prisoner-psychologist story or a prosecutor-defendant-trial story so they skimmed through both narratives rather too briefly. And there were lots of very strangely filmed encounters, a dark nighttime conversation at the Nuremberg rally arena in particular. Russell Crowe was OK, but Rami Malek was poorly cast as the psychologist. And two psychologists in an angry body fight in seemingly empty prison corridor...that would never have happened!

raw_payload.author: shivg
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg (2025) This film works as a gripping and well-made courtroom drama that keeps you invested from start to finish. The tension between the characters, the psychological mind games, and the weight of history all blend together smoothly. The film looks great, sounds great, and maintains a steady pace that never loses your attention.

It doesn't try too hard to be profound, and that's both its strength and its limitation. It's a solid, engaging watch that delivers exactly what it promises but doesn't quite reach the emotional or artistic heights that would make it award-winning.

Overall, a strong 7.5 out of 10 - a polished, well-executed historical drama that is definitely worth watching.

raw_payload.author: barrymw
raw_payload.content:
This is a well told story of a little known part of the Nuremberg trials. It holds your attention right from the off with a well paced script and outstanding performances throughout.

Of course there is a little bit of artistic licence but if I want 100% historical accuracy I would read a book or watch a documentary. As a drama though this works very well.

Using some b&w imagery to give more of a sense of the time, in the trial scenes when actual archive footage of the holocaust is used it all adds to the sense of watching something from the time.

There is one small aspected that irked me a little bit where certain characters are given some dialogue to shoehorn some facts and timeline. It felt a little bit as though the audience weren't trusted enough to know some basic facts. That may well be true but I doubt those people wouldn't be in the audience anyway.

That aside it is a very very good film. What makes it stand apart though are the performances from Russell Crowe and Rami Malek in particular. Michael Shannon also shines and although a smaller part there is one scene in particular where Richard E Grant stands out. I would not be surprised to see award nominations coming soon.

raw_payload.author: steiner-sam
raw_payload.content:
It's a message-laden historical drama about the 1946 Nuremberg trials. We meet Hermann Göring (Russell Crowe) when he is captured after the war, as well as a few lesser lights like Rudolf Hess (Andreas Pietschmann), Julius Streicher (Dieter Riesle), and Robert Ley (Tom Keune), who are brought to trial. One of the movie's prongs follows American Supreme Court Justice Robert Jackson (Michael Shannon), who fights hard for a war crimes trial for which there is no legal precedent. The larger, second prong is the interaction between American psychiatrist Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek) and Hermann Göring, which eventually includes Göring's wife, Emmy (Lotte Verbeek), and daughter, Edda (Fleur Bremmer). Translator Howie Triest (Leo Woodall) is also significant.

"Nuremberg" attempts to do too much, devoting time to Jackson's quest, but tells an incomplete and American-centric story. This is overwhelmed by the more interesting, and closer to the film's message, interaction and near friendship between Kelley and Göring, as Kelley discerns Göring's brilliance and recognizes the danger posed by narcissistic charismatic political leaders. Women have little role in this film, mostly there for "support" and decoration. Even Emmy could have been developed further.

If you're unhappy with the state of politics in America, you may like this "Nuremberg" and wonder why that country has never joined the International Court of Justice, which is modelled on the Nuremberg trials.

raw_payload.author: Danusha_Goska
raw_payload.content: Nuremberg is an entertaining treatment of the trial of top Nazi Hermann Goering. Its aim is to be crowd pleasing but also to touch on serious matters, and it succeeds. Russell Crowe is getting all the attention but Rami Malek worked better for me. John Slattery is commanding. Leo Woodall is attention-getting in a smaller role. He has a scene toward the end of the film that made me cry. There are some inaccuracies. Nuremberg addresses the Nazi persecution of Jews to the exclusion of other victims, including handicapped people, Poles, Soviet POWs, gay people, JWs, and others. But that's a minor complaint about a good movie that manages to both entertain and treat difficult material.

raw_payload.author: Sicilja
raw_payload.content: I had high expectations of this film and it was ok. Felt a bit forced and the dramaturgy was not that good. They could have made it more thrilling even if it's based on a real event. Great acting brings up the grade. They had the opportunity to go deeper regarding where evil comes from but they didn't. You almost felt sympathy with the nazis at the end who faced death.

raw_payload.author: dimitriisov
raw_payload.content:
Paper thin. After all this time, and in this political climate, this movie had nothing original to say about the Holocaust or the Nuremberg trials. The movie had no depth, and the drama was false or undeserving.

The dialogue was highly expository and awkwardly had characters talk about basic facts of WWII and about themselves that they would never actually have verbalized because it should have gone without saying, but the writers felt it necessary to pander to an undereducated audience. Why would a character have to continually remind another character who they are?

The movie was incredibly tonally inconsistent, with characters cracking jokes and then suddenly being despondent. The score would swell for a scene as if there was some kind of dramatic buildup, even though the characters weren't moving the plot forward at all. In multiple scenes, scary music plays while Göring is just doing pushups, as if he's somehow an evil genius preparing to free himself and needs to be in peak condition.

Russell Crowe's performance is totally wasted - his evil does not shine through at all, and the scary music is totally unearned by the script and editing and pacing.

The movie has absolutely zero buildup. The plot meanders around for an hour and then suddenly they're at the trial and the music blares like we were waiting for the trial the whole time. The movie can't pick a lane between a Holocaust movie and a courtroom drama. And then it just peters out after the first day of the trial as if they somehow already won.

Victorious music swells after the first trial day, after Michael Shannon's character fails at questioning Göring and the British prosecutor stands up to take his place and continues his basic questioning for him. Then they both throw meaningful looks at Kelley like he somehow was responsible for them being able to defeat Göring in this scene, even though the evidence they used to counter Göring's testimony has nothing to do with Kelley's psychological profiling and was merely historical fact they recited. Like the psychologist's insight somehow helped them destroy Göring's character, even though when the British prosecutor took over for Michael Shannon to question Göring, he didn't use any of Rami Malek's character's findings whatsoever.

The movie was full of cheesy, cliché scenes - like Michael Shannon and Rami Malek meeting in the rain in trench coats to speak for two minutes about things that would've just taken place in an office, just so they can make a hamfisted reference to a stadium where Hitler gave a speech.

There was extremely poor chemistry between almost all characters, with far too big a cast to devote the amount of time and dialogue needed to develop an emotional connection to the characters. For example, there's an attempted romance between Kelley and a reporter that goes nowhere and has no emotional depth. Douglas Kelley becomes a surrogate father figure to Göring's family out of nowhere. Rami Malek's emotional range in this movie consisted of him staring blankly into space for minutes on end. It didn't feel like the characters actually knew each other for thousands of hours, but like they met on set. Nowhere near enough dialogue was dedicated to feeling an attachment to or betrayal from Göring.

Incredibly sappy scenes where the translator character is crying and Rami Malek is again staring off-screen for like two minutes while the translator Howie (Woodall) is just repeatedly licking and biting his lips because he doesn't have another way to display that he's holding back tears. Not to mention, when he was giving his backstory, no one from Holland back then would have called it Holland - obvious Americanization.

The movie forgets about all the other Nazi characters immediately after introducing them and then randomly brings one of them back near the end.

At one point near the end, Rami Malek's character turns over a King on a deck of cards in an absolutely hamfisted, amateur, bromidic metaphor for Göring.  Watch famous moments from the real trial instead.

raw_payload.author: CP-737
raw_payload.content: Movie was great however not fully accurate and a bit too much drama. I was impressed by Malek's role as Freddie in Queen however in every single role since it seems he is a check mark, i.e., doesn't fit the character in appearance nor the role. I truly wish Hollywood would stop placing individuals in roles who simply don't fit.

raw_payload.author: rasmus-608-635081
raw_payload.content: Russel Crowe plays well (probaly rehearsed eating burgers;-) Malik in one of his weakest performances. raw_payload.author: Sees All
raw_payload.content: I thought NUREMBERG was terrific on all levels. Most important is that it's solid entertainment, very well done. The script was intelligent, suspenseful, and provocative. It's basically a psychological game of cat-and-mouse between two crafty adversaries, top-Nazi (now that Hitler is dead) Hermann Goering versus the Allies' top psychiatrist Douglas Kelley as they try to prepare their testimony and cross-examination for the War Crimes Trial in the wake of World War II. They come to like and respect each other while at the same time trying win at any cost. For Goering his very life is at stake, for Kelley it's making certain that the Nazis could in no way be exonerated. They are both brilliant strategists who will do anything to win. The result is a gripping movie chock-full of great performances. There's Oscar potential for both Russell Crowe as Goering and Rami Malek as Kelley. They are given sensational support by Michael Shannon, John Slattery, Leo Woodall, and my old favorite, Richard E. Grant. Top notch writing and direction are by James Vanderbilt (whose previous credits I'm unaware of). The cinematography at first bothered me because of the use of a blue-green filter that was popular about 10 years ago, but it probably did make it easier to interpolate old newsreel footage. Also must mention the excellent music by Brian Tyler, which served to heighten the emotions without being obtrusive. For me, this is a 10-best movie I've seen in a long time and I'm a frequent moviegoer.

raw_payload.author: jstalmer
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg is described as a courtroom drama, which is true in the narrowest sense and misleading in every way that matters. Guilt is not in question here. The facts are established. The verdicts are inevitable. What the film is actually interested in is something far more unsettling: what happens to belief after defeat, and why certainty survives even when history collapses beneath it.

I've always loved courtroom dramas and films about journalists for the same reason. People in rooms, trying to connect the dots. Evidence contested. Truth circling but never landing cleanly. When these films are done well, they're sublime. When they're not, they're unbearable. Nuremberg understands exactly why this form works and then uses it to ask a different question than we're used to.

The smartest storytelling decision is the vantage point. Instead of centering prosecutors or turning the trial into moral theater, the film orbits the prison psychiatrist. His role is not to judge or absolve, but to listen. To observe. To record. That choice shifts the entire weight of the story. The question stops being "Did they do it?" and becomes "How did they live with themselves while doing it?" and, more disturbingly, "Why do so many of them still believe they were right?"

That's the part that really stays with you. Hitler is dead. The war is lost. The evidence is overwhelming. And yet many of these men remain fully committed. Still loyal. Still rationalizing. Still certain. Watching that unfold is chilling not because it's shocking, but because it's familiar. Human nature rarely resets when circumstances change. Belief protects identity, and identity is defended long after it becomes indefensible.

The film is also honest about something uncomfortable. Yes, this was a trial. But it was also, in many ways, a foregone conclusion. What was going to happen had been decided before the proceedings began. That doesn't make the trial meaningless, but it does make it complicated. Justice here isn't discovery. It's reckoning. Public acknowledgment. The film never pretends otherwise, and that refusal to manufacture suspense is one of its strengths.

This is where Nuremberg quietly aligns itself with thinkers like Hannah Arendt. Evil is not presented as madness or theatrical villainy. It's administrative. Bureaucratic. Certain. The psychiatrist feels less like a healer than an anthropologist, cataloging a worldview that persists even when stripped of power. From a psychological standpoint, the film plays like a case study in cognitive dissonance. When belief is threatened by overwhelming evidence, people don't abandon it. They reinforce it. They protect it. They refine it. Certainty becomes a shelter long after it stops being defensible.

The film inevitably brushes up against questions of capital punishment. Listening to these men justify, intellectualize, and deflect, you can't help but ask what execution actually accomplishes. Does it dismantle belief, or does it simply end the conversation?

What makes all of this work is the discipline of the filmmaking itself. The directing never pushes for emphasis it hasn't earned. The camera doesn't argue, it observes. The casting is exact, not showy, and that precision matters. Performances are controlled, interior, and unnervingly calm, which allows belief to reveal itself without being dramatized into caricature. The production design and locations feel lived-in and claustrophobic without announcing themselves. Nothing pulls focus. Everything serves the same quiet insistence: stay with this. Pay attention.

There is something refreshingly rare about how this film is made. We don't get enough movies like this anymore. It is dark, but not sensational. Serious without being heavy-handed. Confident enough to trust rooms, silences, and faces instead of spectacle. It feels like a film that could have been made in the 1970s, not because of nostalgia, but because of restraint. The script assumes attention rather than begging for it. The acting is precise, not demonstrative. That quality matters. It creates space for thought.

What Nuremberg refuses to offer is catharsis. There is no redemption arc. No hero who makes things right. Compare it to Judgment at Nuremberg, which believes in the courtroom as a moral instrument, or Schindler's List, which finds meaning in individual goodness. Nuremberg goes somewhere colder and, in its own way, more honest. It suggests that exposure may be the only ethical outcome, and that bearing witness is sometimes the work itself.

By the time the film ends, you're left with a feeling rather than a verdict. Not closure, but recognition. History is not black and white. It never is. It's made of people, belief systems, rationalizations, and fear. The most dangerous thing in the room isn't rage. It's certainty.

In the end, Nuremberg isn't about justice being served. It's about the mind that made justice necessary in the first place. It's about belief surviving defeat, and the uncomfortable truth that paying attention may be the most honest response when resolution is impossible.

That's why the film lingers.

Not because it explains the past.

But because it refuses to let you forget how the present gets made.

Rating: 8.8.

raw_payload.author: FiftyTwo_52
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg succeeds primarily because it refuses to be simplistic.

Russell Crowe and Rami Malek deliver rock-solid performances that carry the film through its quieter, talkier stretches, lending gravity and tension to what could have been dry courtroom material. What intrigued me most was not the verdicts, but the film's willingness to question the process itself.

I'm no expert on the precise historical accuracy of the trials, but if this depiction reflects the core prosecution strategy, it feels uncomfortably close to a kangaroo court - a point the film seems aware of.

More interestingly, it hints at how American propaganda shaped a clean, unidimensional narrative for decades, while quietly exposing moral compromises beneath the surface. That subtext, and its resonance with today's polarized U. S. politics, gives the film real bite.

Minor pacing issues aside, this is an engaging and thought-provoking watch.

raw_payload.author: Sleepin_Dragon
raw_payload.content:
Douglas Kelley is brought in to carry out psychological assessments on officers prior to the Nuremberg trials, and forms an interesting bond with Hermann Göring.

After one of the most disappointing summers of films imaginable, the year did at least end with something of a flourish, and the best of the recent releases for me is Nuremberg.

I was expecting a remake of Judgment at Nuremberg, but this takes a very different perspective. It's a film that sits alongside the classic adaptation, but happily looks up to it. It doesn't hold back on detailing the atrocities either, and there is some genuinely harrowing stock footage.

The acting is good to excellent across the board, however it's Russell Crowe who steals the film. He is outstanding as Göring, delivering a huge performance, boosted by his imposing frame. He's conceited, defiant, the ultimate narcissist, with the voice and accent spot on.

The casting in general is strong, with some particularly good supporting performances. I thought Michael Shannon was excellent. The visuals are impressive too, especially the realisation of a destroyed city.

I'd suggest this is one of the best films of the year. How nice it is to watch a new movie in 2025 and be able to say: brilliant, if very sobering.

9/10.

raw_payload.author: zkonedog
raw_payload.content:
There are two ways to make a historical film such as Nuremberg. The first is to take some liberties (if more in time than in actual facts/events) in pursuit of drama. The second is the "just the facts, ma'am" strategy. Director/writer James Vanderbilt takes the latter approach here, which crimps the movie's ceiling but also raises its floor.

For a very basic overview, this film looks at the Nuremberg Trials largely from the perspective of Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek), a psychiatrist brought in to evaluate the German prisoners, most notably Hermann Goring (Russell Crowe). Simultaneously, Justice Robert H. Jackson (Michael Shannon) struggles to put together a war crimes trial unlike anything any justice system has ever seen.

I'll start with the ceiling-limitation on Nuremberg, which is that it's far from Spielberg-ian drama. This is a simply-made film that does not lean into dramatic flourishes. This approach renders it more "history lesson" than "fully engrossing drama". As such, it's tough to give Nuremberg the highest of marks.

On the positive side of things, Nuremberg covers a compelling story which was truly a watershed moment in the history of war and its crimes. Crowe is chilling in embodying the pompous Goring, while Malek portrays a wonderfully sympathetic adversary. The final 20-30 minutes are the highlight here, when the psychological battling and the actual trial converge.

All told, Nuremberg is a movie that deftly portrays all the themes it sets out to cover. The underlying message, that fascism isn't contained to the Nazi regime, is an important one (if frustratingly difficult to convey with high drama and still be as historically accurate as Vanderbilt shoots for here). This results in somewhat of a "take your medicine and learn your history" feel to the overall experience (eschewing manufactured drama), but the end result is still a solid flick from which at least some entertainment and many lessons can be derived.

raw_payload.author: arthur_tafero

Director James Vanderbilt, along with writer Jack El-Hai, both did an outstanding job of re-creating the courtroom atmosphere of the world-famous trial of the century in 1946. I found the segment of Hermann Hess fascinating, and thought it should have been examined more closely, but then the film would have been an unwieldy three hours.

There are several top-notch performances in this film. Outside of Malek's Oscar-winning performance, there is Russell Crowe's Oscar-winning performance. Leo Woodall and John Slattery (Madmen) also give great performances.

So, aside from Best Film, Best Editing, Best Screenplay, Best Actor, and Best Supporting Actor, this film should win at least 5 Academy Awards (probably more), or I don't know anything about reviewing films. I will go out on a limb on this one. Do not miss this one; it is EASILY the best film of 2025.

raw_payload.author: AshleyO-699

I believed, going into a picture that depicts arguably the most important historically relevant trial in history would go one of two ways, either it would be depleted of actual facts and simplified or it would be overly complex and go over most people's heads. I'll give it some credit as for the most part it finds somewhat of a middle ground. It lays out some of the critical points surrounding the trial and it's main players but the picture does cater for a wider audience than a fact based drama would settle for. Personally I think the studio and writers of this movie have made the assumption that us viewers couldn't handle some of the complex issues surrounding this trial, and I think they have assumed wrong.

That's not to say that this is not a great movie, it's pretty well executed, but this is for the masses so those more familiar with the trial and it's characters may find fault throughout. But I'll try my best to look at this objectively as just a movie.

This movie is one of hundreds when it comes to depictions of Nazi's in a trend that although overcrowded is still important, so for me personally I welcomed the prospect of another insight into the atrocities because it should be talked about and not forgotten. The Director James Vanderbilt does a great job at not just bringing this to screen but cramming quite a lot of information into this two and a half hour picture, it's paced pretty well in a picture that could of easily been a slow burn and overdrawn.

But this was definitely one of those pictures that could of easily gone one way or another with it's depictions of characters. And I have nothing but praise for Russell Crowe who is quite brilliant as Hermann Goring in his most commanding performance since 'Gladiator', there is talk of Oscar buzz around his performance, while I don't think he will win, I have no doubt it's a strong nomination contender.

raw_payload.author: TalahaseesLittleBrother
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg (2025) is a compelling and timely historical drama that brings fresh attention to one of the most significant legal and moral reckonings in modern history: the Nuremberg Trials. Directed by James Vanderbilt and arriving on the 80th anniversary of those proceedings, the film explores the psychological cat-and-mouse game between U. S. Army psychiatrist Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek) and high-ranking Nazi Hermann Göring (Russell Crowe), while weaving in the broader efforts to hold the defeated regime accountable for its atrocities.

Russell Crowe delivers a standout performance as Göring, capturing the character's charisma, cunning manipulation, and chilling self-assurance with commanding presence. His portrayal is sly, larger-than-life yet grounded, making the Reichsmarschall a genuinely formidable antagonist who remains unsettlingly human. Crowe's work ranks among his strongest in recent years, anchoring the film with intensity and nuance that elevate every scene he's in.

The movie's core message resonates powerfully today: the importance of international justice, the dangers of unchecked authoritarianism, and the chilling reality that the roots of evil can take hold anywhere if society fails to confront them. By dramatizing the establishment of accountability for war crimes and crimes against humanity, Nuremberg serves as a stark reminder that such horrors must never be repeated, making it feel urgently relevant in our current global climate.

That said, the film is not without its flaws. Rami Malek's portrayal of Kelley often feels uneven-some moments land with emotional weight, but others come across as overly mannered or detached, with twitchy tics and enigmatic smiles that border on theatrical excess. It doesn't quite match the gravitas Crowe brings, creating an imbalance in their central dynamic.

Additionally, the script takes liberties with history, compressing timelines, simplifying complex events, and introducing dramatic flourishes that stray from documented facts. These historical and technical inaccuracies-along with occasional pacing issues-prevent the film from achieving full depth or authenticity.

Overall, Nuremberg is a worthwhile and thought-provoking watch: handsomely crafted, powerfully acted in key roles, and deeply resonant in its warning about the fragility of justice and humanity. It's timely and important, even if it's not flawless. If you're interested in WWII history, moral accountability, or strong lead performances, this is worth seeing-despite its imperfections.

raw_payload.author: lizloveswrite
raw_payload.content:
After reading some of these reviews, it's a mistake to compare this film with Kubrick's excellent film because this film isn't focused on the Nuremberg trials as courtroom drama. The main focus is on Göring & the "relationship" he develops with Dr. Kelley, & how Kelley comes to realize the depths of evil Göring's capable of committing behind his often charming, intelligent, narcissistic--and sometimes disarming--facade, & that this evil isn't a trait of a "Nazi mind." A secondary focus relates to the difficult task of establishing trials when no precedent existed for such trials; the allies weren't even sure what crimes to charge the Nazi leaders with, but it was absolutely necessary that once charged & tried, the Nazi leaders had to be convicted because the world was watching. That sounds simple. Who could imagine them not being convicted? But it wasn't as simple as it seems decades later; Göring made escaping justice not outside the realm of possibility.

Crowe is simply amazing as Göring: alternately haughty, defiant, warm, funny, & even vulnerable--but always there's an underlying menacing aspect to him. He has great affection for his family, for his country, for his leader, but he can casually sign off on the death of millions of people & feel no remorse nor regret. And he is always in control of himself.

Malik is also good, but sometimes his reactions are, imo, exaggerated. He gets reeled in by Göring, & it's not until he physically sees the results of Göring's actions that he truly sees the real Göring.

The entire supporting cast was top notch, most notably Michael Shannon as Justice Jackson, a man of principle, who was the driving force behind the tribunal, & Leo Woodall as Sgt. Triest. (There's been some criticism of John Slatterly, but he captures Colonel Andrus.) It's easy to see how Göring, & those like him, manipulated & captured an entire nation's people given the devastation to Germany after WW I. It's also easy to see that this type of manipulation didn't end with the Third Reich.

raw_payload.author: JordanShaheen
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg is one of those films that reminds you just how powerful great casting can be. With performances from Rami Malek, Russell Crowe, John Slattery, Leo Woodall, and a handful of other strong supporting names, the movie feels stacked from the moment it opens. And despite its nearly three-hour runtime, it never drags. Instead, it holds you in this tense, intellectual grip from start to finish.

Russell Crowe delivers one of his most unsettling performances in years as Hermann Göring. He captures the charisma, the self-importance, and the chilling delusion that defined the man-making him both fascinating and repulsive at the same time. Rami Malek matches him beat for beat as Dr. Kelley, the psychiatrist who enters the prison confident and cocky, only to slowly unravel as he comes face-to-face with a type of narcissism and manipulation he's clearly not prepared for. Their dynamic is the backbone of the film, and it plays almost like a subtle psychological thriller rather than a traditional historical drama.

One of the film's biggest strengths is the way it forces the viewer to wrestle with an uncomfortable idea: do evil men retain a human side, or do ordinary humans all carry a capacity for evil? The script doesn't preach either message directly, but it places you right in the tension of those questions. It humanizes Göring just long enough to make you uneasy-right before reminding you in disturbing detail exactly what he was responsible for. That emotional whiplash seems entirely intentional, and it lingers.

The movie also shines a light on a part of World War II history that isn't taught in depth in most American classrooms: the Nuremberg trials themselves. It brings the legal, ethical, and psychological stakes into sharp focus. At the same time, the film isn't shy about drawing modern parallels, reminding us how easily societies can be swayed by narcissistic leaders and how devastating it can be to underestimate them-something Dr. Kelley learns the hard way.

All that said, Nuremberg still lives in a category with an enormous number of World War II films. And while it's well-acted, engaging, and absolutely worth watching, it doesn't quite reach the heights of the very best entries in the genre. It's a strong film-not a masterpiece.

Final rating: 7/10. A compelling, uncomfortable, and well-performed historical drama that earns your time, even if it doesn't fully set itself apart.
