And Kelly the brilliant but misunderstood psychiatrist who is the only one who has the key to convicting Göring.

Then the casting: Malek is.... well, very poor. For some reason he has a weird grin on his face in literally every scene, as if he still thinks he is the Bond villan. Casting Crowe as Göring in itself is already odd, plenty of excellent German actors available who can also speak English, but his Russian-like accent in English spoils every scene for me (not to mention his accent in German).

The mixture of fake b&w footage witch actual historical footage is a big no no in this era of fake videos and AI. I even heard (older) people comment on the fake b&w courtroom scenes stating that these were the actual clips from the trial!

Also, the dramatic tone of every scene starts to annoy very quickly. I went with a friend and we both agreed: had we watched this on netflix, we would have turned it of after 20 minutes or so. Now we had to sit through this overlong and boring mess.

The good thing: at least not everything is in English (yes, Valkyrie, I am looking at you).

raw_payload.author: lsimonsulzer
raw_payload.content: Besides the description and the breathtaking incarnation of huge characters by the actors; besides a perfect and clear script; besides the reconstitution of the trial, the movie offers many layers of interpretation and real food for questions about how it was possible and how it is still possible nowadays for human beings to commit such atrocities. The Nuremberg trial did not stop them by showing the world the depth of horrors.

raw_payload.author: fabomakedonija
raw_payload.content:
When Hannah Arendt wrote that the Nazis were not pure evil, but rather grey mice on a bureaucratic mission to fulfill their duties to the Führer, it was shocking. People did not want to see how ordinary individuals could be capable of committing acts of pure evil.

Today, eighty years after the trial, people still refuse to see the danger. This movie therefore isn't an ordinary trial film, nor a simple story of good versus evil, but a clear warning. For that reason, it doesn't just deserve a 7 out of 10, but a 9.

Why? Because the director did not choose the easy Hollywood narrative. This is not a story of American heroes, but a story of honesty and introspection. It may be one of the few honest Hollywood films. One that haunts your mind, one that makes you think and rethink.

Don't say you didn't know.

raw_payload.author: mrglenngrant
raw_payload.content:
Russell Crowe. What an actor. This film would score a lot less were it not for him.

The story was quite fascinating. I don't feel they dug deeply enough into why they wanted a trial though. It was blatantly obvious what the outcome was going to be so it all seemed to be a bit of a charade.

Rami Malick was annoying but Crowe and Shannon were really excellent. Did RC live on donuts for a month to put on all that weight?

At one point I thought that Malick's character was going to end up with Goering's wife! They were getting pretty, pretty close there.

I would have liked some explanation of how he managed to get a cyanide capsule.

71/100.

raw_payload.author: HenryH-589
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg is absolutely amazing from the very start to the very end. Powerhouse performances throughout from Remi Malek, Russell Crowe, Colin Hanks and Richard E Grant.

The film follows the events at the end of World War II and the Nuremberg trials. It is emotional but an absolute must watch! I would be surprised if Oscar nominations don't follow.

9/10.

raw_payload.author: gabriel-80
raw_payload.content: It seems like all the ingredients are there, and I know it reflects real events, but it just doesn't get very exiting. Most of the nazis remain anonymous. Russel Crowe is truly great though. You forget that it is him. Would have liked a deeper study of the psychology of his Göhring character. Was he really "just" an ideologist, not evil per say, was he a narcissist (as the movie states), was he sympathetic or just manipulative? I don't really feel like I get an answer to that, and I find that truly interesting - especially in this day and age where it feels like we are in a new age of despots and dictators.

raw_payload.author: titusvari
raw_payload.content:
The central theme of "Nuremberg" is more relevant today than most would want to admit: evil doesn't need a scary uniform; it can look like us. Russell Crowe's performance is the film's strongest asset, firmly deserving of an Oscar. He captures the complexity of Hermann Göring-a husband and father who was also a master manipulator, eventually pulling off one final "trick" to escape the gallows on his own terms.

While Crowe dominates the screen, both Göring and Kelley are remarkably deep historical figures. The film portrays Douglas Kelley as an "artist" of psychiatry who was misunderstood and ahead of his time. His intellectual struggle to categorize the men in the dock provides the film's psychological weight.

The film highlights the fact that both men eventually ended their lives in the exact same manner. This identical end leaves room for reflection: it may have been a final, silent attempt by Kelley to ensure that his work and his findings on the nature of the human mind were finally taken seriously.

This film is a necessary observation of human patterns. It serves as a reminder that the behaviors witnessed in 1945 are not confined to the past;

raw_payload.author: wellthatswhatithinkanyway
raw_payload.content:
STAR RATING: ***** Brilliant **** Very Good *** Okay ** Poor * Awful

Hermann Goring (Russell Crowe), Hitler's second in command, is caught fleeing after the downfall of his evil regime by American Allied Troops, along with a group of other Nazi high commanders. Justice Robert H. Jackson (Michael Shannon) is caught in a legal limbo, unable to put the men on trial for crimes not committed on American soil, while America was not involved in the war. Eventually, a compromise is reached, and psychological analyst Douglas Kelly (Rami Malek) is called in to assess Goring, as the main Nazi chief, but ends up forming an uncomfortable relatability with his nature.

It's a jumbled modern filmic landscape we operate in, with so many premieres on the various streaming services, or more conventional subscription channels. I don't recall watching anything on Sky Atlantic before, but now this original production of theirs has wound up on the big screen, where it has some legitimacy. James Vanderbilt's sprawling account of the Nuremberg trials has been covered before, but he still delivers a worthy if not entirely fulfilling dramatization of his own.

In spite of the all star cast, all eyes are still on Crowe, in the lead role as the portly Nazi chief, and delivers an absorbing performance, nailing the accent and mannerisms of the monster, but not leaving out the charming front he was capable of putting on, humanising a beast we could all become if the moon and stars aligned, which co star Malek finds himself uncomfortably drawn in by in his role. Sadly, despite these decent performances, the script possesses a lack of dynamism in spite of the weight of the theme, with a fairly slow, dull pace at the beginning, which does become more intense and powerful towards the end.

It's easy to look at the worst of us, and disassociate ourselves from them as though they were some deadly germ, but it's equally easy to forget that they are ultimately the same flesh and blood as us, and it's important to understand them as people for this reason. Vanderbilt's film is an uncomfortable, compelling, if not entirely delivering effort, ultimately still well worth seeing. *** and a half.

raw_payload.author: alhudaithyb
raw_payload.content: Nuremberg (2025) is a masterclass in psychological tension, earning a 10/10 for its acting. The film is driven by the electric, high-stakes duel between Russell Crowe, who is terrifyingly charismatic as Hermann Göring, and Rami Malek, who captures the haunting descent of psychiatrist Douglas Kelley. With Michael Shannon adding a volcanic intensity to the courtroom, it is a rare historical drama where the performances are as explosive as any action scene.

raw_payload.author: movieman6-413-929510
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg is a new historical war drama, directed, written, and partly produced by James Vanderbilt, for whom this is only his second time in the director's chair, following Truth from 2015.

In 1945, shortly after the fall of Adolf Hitler and the Nazi Third Reich, German political and military leader Hermann Göring surrenders to the American army. Prosecutor Justice Robert H. Jackson (Michael Shannon) wants to give him the fairest trial possible, hoping to prevent future global conflicts.

Psychiatrist Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek) is assigned to evaluate Göring in order to determine whether he is mentally fit to stand trial for his war crimes - crimes that Göring partly denies, or claims to know nothing about.

This is an interesting film, one that attempts to show the brutality of the Second World War and the suffering inflicted upon the Jewish people. Despite overwhelming evidence, some Nazis continued to deny or feign ignorance of their actions. Because Germany had been heavily punished after the First World War - a factor that contributed to the rise of the Second World War - America and the other Allied nations chose not to impose an equally harsh penalty after 1945. However, the higher-ranking Nazis still had to face the consequences of their actions and the horrific aftermath they caused.

Although James Vanderbilt does not yet have extensive experience as a director, he has considerably more background as a writer - though his work has previously been focused mainly on action and horror films. A serious war drama is therefore new territory for him. This occasionally shows, particularly when the film suddenly shifts into lighter comedic moments. These scenes can feel misplaced, especially in contrast with the later sequences that present real archival footage of concentration camp victims - raw and confronting material.

The film also feels somewhat overcrowded, as it follows too many characters. Besides the psychiatrist and Göring, the story also focuses on the prosecutor's preparations, the examination of other Nazi prisoners, and even internal rivalry between psychiatrists and prosecutors. These shifting perspectives often result in characters overshadowing one another, which at times leads to confusion or a dragging pace. Had the story focused more exclusively on the psychological cat-and-mouse game between Douglas and Göring, the narrative might have felt tighter and more impactful.

Russell Crowe gives a compelling performance as a Nazi figurehead who, despite defeat, clings to his pride or simply continues to deny the reality of what happened. A future award nomination would be well-deserved. Rami Malek and Michael Shannon also deliver strong work, but due to the constant narrative shifts, the actors sometimes overshadow each other. As a result, the time spent on one of them can in hindsight feel somewhat unnecessary.

raw_payload.author: CriticaSinFiltro
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg 2025" doesn't fail because of its cast, budget or production value - it fails because it's afraid of its audience.

The film retells the most important trial of the 20th century with the tone of a modern lightweight drama, simplifying characters, motivations and historical context until almost nothing remains of the real Tribunal.

Douglas Kelley is rewritten as a TV-friendly protagonist, crucial historical figures like Gustave Gilbert are erased, and invented episodes (such as the Vatican subplot) replace the actual moral and political tension of the time.

These choices reveal a fundamental distrust in the viewer's ability to engage with complexity.

By softening the tragedy and turning the trial into an accessible "streaming-style" narrative, the film loses not only accuracy, but respect - for the victims, the history, and the meaning of the Nuremberg process itself.

A missed opportunity to confront the truth with the weight it deserves.

raw_payload.author: buttercuplake
raw_payload.content: Outstanding Performance by Russell Crowe and Rami Malek. This movie was well written and directed. Very powerful stuff my eyes were stuck to the screen for the entire film. The world indeed needs to wake up. If you think this will not happen again you are blind. The Netanyahu/Trump era is upon us and it is scary. The Evil that men do.

raw_payload.author: ajkbiotech
raw_payload.content:
This epic, three hour film was outstanding.

Meticulously directed, beautifully filmed, intelligently executed, intense performances from an all star cast, this film is a wonderful and engaging, evocative pièce de résistance of the famous WW2 trials.

Russell Crowe, Rami Malek are brilliant. So is the rest of the cast.

A must see.

raw_payload.author: Filip_Pruncu
raw_payload.content:
Don't get me wrong, the movie is not bad at all. It's good. The actors do a great job. It just doesn't raise to the high expectations for such an important subject.

Russell Crowe and Rami Malek did a great job. Michael Shannon didn't shine like he shined in other movies but he was still good. However, I was impressed by Leo Woodall's acting. It's the first movie I see him in and I'm pleased with his performance. Good job! The other actors didn't get a chance to shine, they didn't appear on screen enough to make an impression.

The dialogue is good, but could've been more deep considering who is talking to who and about what. It should've been more intense, more intelligent. I found it a bit flat.

Even though it's not a short film, it keeps you entertained, has a good pace and you don't get bored, but you're not on the edge of your seat either.

I don't want to get into history because this is not a documentary, but one thing that lacked and should've been portrayed at least a bit was the Russian influence in the trial, which was not small. The trial itself was based mostly on documentation provided by the Soviet Union.

I guess the director James Vanderbilt carries a lot of the blame, since he just doesn't have enough experience with movies of such magnitude. I believe this is his second directorial position, he's usually a producer. This movie deserved a genius of a director.

There are many movies on the nazis, and while this one is different from them, I guess people had enough of the same subject. It should've set it apart, but somehow it doesn't. It had a big chance to be one of the greatest movies about the subject.

raw_payload.author: 2ReL4u
raw_payload.content: It's amazing how Russell Crowe plays "the bad guy"in roles that you tend to side with 3 :10 to Yuma as Ben Wade for example. This movie took me back in time like i was in the courtroom. With dialogue and delivery that had my full attention from Crowe. It was horrible what happened and i was left wishing this movie was fictional.

raw_payload.author: jetsetter311-540-775830
raw_payload.content:
I read the book in anticipation of this film and the book (while well written) left me wanting as it only scratched the surface as though the author only had limited evidence to carry the topic...Kelley versus Göring and the impact the latter had on the former.

I felt the film was superficial too and played at achieving the same dynamic as between Clarice Starling and Hannibal Lecter. I had really hoped the film would do what films based on books do, take some creative liberties and really get into Kelley's psyche. Only it didn't!

I wonder if I would be hailing the film as a masterpiece if I hadn't read the book but I guess I'll never know. It's like I say though, even when a WW2 film is bad, it's still good. Keeping history at the forefront.

raw_payload.author: kassdo
raw_payload.content: It was a good movie for a 2025 audience looking at a critical moment in history, with zero knowledge. It provides basic, but if interested, do you own research. There are some nasty in that jail. Having seen JaN as a a 15 years after release, that is the benchmark film for this story. The 2025 acting was very good but the story always seemed everywhere with themes at surface level. And the film like most docudramas today had a disclosure about reality. Some scene seemed far fetch so the conversation about Hiroshima being a war crime in 1946 is far fetched but perfect for revisionist 2025 judging WW2 allies decisions. Very torn on the movie, particular the US Army Psychiatrist behavior. For me it was not a happy ending, but I would recommend viewing along with Judgement at Nuremberg. Spencer Tracy shows his acting chops.

raw_payload.author: mike-nissley
raw_payload.content: Sweeping music cues, hackneyed camera shots, plenty of time out from the main story for emotional moments, and gee whiz aren't the good guys compelled by unshakeable decency and greatness of purpose? Yawn. Shannon and especially Crowe are great, with Crowe doing an outstanding job. Malek by turns overdoes it then underdoes it. Not his best work, just odd. The story is thin given the subject matter. It is really long and doesn't need to be. A handful of blank and stereotypical second and third players are peppered throughout. Thoroughly average expensive looking Hollywood movie with bells and whistles loud as can be, but not much heft. Forgettable other than Russel Crowe in a fantastic performance.

raw_payload.author: mmazzie
raw_payload.content:
You cannot see this movie without thinking that what Trump is doing to the U. S. is exactly what Hitler and Himmler were trying to accomplish in Germany. Great supporting cast.

Excellent acting by the entire cast! Very sharp script and direction. Russel Crowe and Rami Malik were terrific..great view of post-war Germany.

raw_payload.author: fmdead
raw_payload.content:
I had high expectations for this movie, expecting a taut, cat-and-mouse mind game between the psychiatrist and Goering. It started off well then just unravelled for me, seeming more and more implausible. For example, the psychiatrist visits Goering's wife and daughter multiple times, and even exchanges letters between them and Goering, and supposedly the military doesn't even know about it? When it involves the most highly guarded prisoner in the world? (maybe this actually happened, but it beggars belief.) Early on, the psychiatrist advises the lawyer that the way to sink Goering at trial is to play to his pride and ego - but nothing of the sort happened, there was no payback to that story angle. Instead, supposedly the psychiatrist's private notes were the key to Goering losing his case, but they actually played no part in the courtroom climax. There are many other "huh?" moments in this film.

Russell Crowe is great but let down by the emptiness of the script. Rami Malek is a bundle of smirking, wise-cracking self-confidence, which is at odds with the seriousness of the situation he's in. The time he spends with Goering doesn't evidence any real psychological analysis going on either. The side characters' acting I thought was weak and unconvincing.

I debated rating this film a seven because it does capture the look and feel of the era and of course any replay of the horrors and atrocities of WWII and the people responsible for them is very sobering. However, this movie falls far short of its ambitions.

raw_payload.author: dguest-57663
raw_payload.content:
A film on an important subject isn't automatically an important film. It doesn't become so with the addition of portentous music. If anything, that makes it suspiciously self-important.

You're left to wonder whether this might be a court-room drama with so little drama that music reminiscent of the Terminator films has to be hauled in to bulk it up.

That's not to say 'Nuremberg' is a bad film. Russell Crowe as Göring is OK, and Rami Malek only descends into mumbling after being thumped in the mouth by another senior Nazi. But nobody feels quite substantial enough for the Trial of the Century. Worse, the suggestion that Göring's conviction depended on a clunky prosecutorial sleight-of-hand risks trivialising the matter.

If you want to watch a very good film, with real movie stars (and William Shatner), a much better script and a more subtle treatment of hugely important issues, watch 'Judgment at Nuremberg' instead.

raw_payload.author: daniju
raw_payload.content:
This propulsive and intelligent film arrives at the multiplex like a burst of fresh air. Or rather, a burst of 90s air.

For me, the 90s as a decade was a time of intelligent yet accessible and crowd pleasing movies. A time where an adult orientated film could be made, filled with "large" performances and with something to say.

A time when Spielberg could make Schindler's List, Michael Mann could make The Insider, and Ron Howard was churning out dramas such as A Beautiful Mind.

Some good. Some forgettable, but aimed at adults and never trying to alienate its wide audience.

Compare that to the "big" films today such as One Battle After Another, Anora, or Bugonia, and the deliberate attempt to be niche and extreme, is really evident.

This film, far from perfect, had that classic vibe. Strangely enough so did Aronovsky's Caught Stealing a few months back, albeit in a very different film.

Nuremberg just felt refreshingly grown up.

Didn't really rate the performance of Malik as he seemed rather miscast, and the pacing was variable, but Michael Shannon was reminiscent of a Gregory Peck stoicism, and as for Russel Crowe, I felt he was fantastic.

Again, sad that back in the day, he'd have been talked up as an Oscar winner along the lines of Dr Lector, people would have been discussing his portrayal, whereas today, the film won't even make a ripple and will be on streaming within weeks whereupon it will disappear.

But I really do urge you to seek this film out on the big screen. It's worth the ticket price.

raw_payload.author: BurzaMiklosGR44
raw_payload.content: And the Oscar goes to Mr. Russell Crow! Amazing over amazing performance! I do stuck my seat today in Regal cinemas Seattle ( fan 12 ) incredible movie ! The total movie goes out like a split secound! The role G whit mr Crow like for real ! The sunglass on him at the court room was ICE COLD ! Its a must see masterpiece! Perfekt performance!

raw_payload.author: harmonyzoesugar
raw_payload.content:
I watched Nuremberg with my mom, and we were both confused by the tone of the film, especially in the first half. The atmosphere often felt strangely light for such a heavy subject. There were moments that used almost comedic pacing or a playful tone, which didn't fit the seriousness of the theme. Malik's portrayal of Dr. Kelly came across as somewhat goofy, and that made it hard to take the early parts of the story seriously. It felt like the film wanted us to be shocked as Dr. Kelly slowly became more aware of the truth-but because the tone started off so light, the emotional shift didn't land as strongly as it should have.

We also didn't appreciate how often the film explained things too directly. There were many scenes where characters stated the obvious or asked for assessments of situations that were already very clear to the viewer. Instead of trusting the audience to understand what was happening, the film kept spelling it out, which made parts of it feel shallow. The story had great potential, but it lacked depth. The ending, in particular, could have been much more powerful if we had been more emotionally connected to Dr. Kelly's inner struggle and his process of coming to terms with what he discovered.

That said, the acting was good, and the story itself is very interesting and important to tell. The film did a strong job presenting historical facts and helping viewers understand the context of the events. That part was genuinely appreciated, especially since not everyone starts the film with detailed knowledge of this period. Overall, Nuremberg is informative and well-acted, but its inconsistent tone and lack of emotional depth kept it from reaching its full potential.

raw_payload.author: sbbostick
raw_payload.content: At first glance this movie seems to be another dramatic account of one of the most confusing and horrific times in history. Like other films have done, it tracks the trials of the greatest evil that we have experienced in modern times. However, it takes it a step deeper, narrowing its focus on tones of humanity and evil, and if one's presence negates the others. Russel Crowe phenomenally portrays a Nazi that encapsulates the intelligence and wit of a likable human, but has the sinister power unlike never seen before. Supported by the other actors, this film curiously depicts different perspectives in society grappling with this tragic event tying in personal, logical, moral, and spiritual conflict. Are the Nazi's sinister at heart or were they more or less embedded in something too far gone unbeknownst to them until it was too late? Is there a moral code to live by across all nations that can be agreed upon in the absence of case law? How can humans make things right when all options seem inexplicably wrong? These are a few questions that came to mind when watching this film. It is extremely solemn towards the end, but one that people need to see. It explains the horrors of the world, and forces the viewer to consider how to react. With a few minor issues like pacing, this movie will turn out to be considered art of humanity. Not because it tells a fantastical story, but because it gives the account of an event most want to forget in some sense. It pains to witness the world at its worst, but it allows room to encounter the world at its finest. This movie will turn out to be in discussion for years to come.

raw_payload.author: dierregi
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg reminds us that even a subject as exhaustively covered as World War II, still has corners worth revisiting. Rather than replaying the horrors, the film focuses on the psychological evaluations of Nazi leaders before the trials, centring on the uneasy relationship between Hermann Göring and American psychiatrist Douglas Kelley.

What unfolds is an unsettling dynamic: Kelley, meant to assess Göring's sanity, becomes increasingly fascinated by him, blurring professional boundaries by running errands and delivering letters to Göring's family. Rami Malek gives Kelley an appropriate nervous intensity, suggesting a man both curious and dangerously impressionable.

Russell Crowe dominates the film as Göring. Without heavy prosthetics, he delivers a controlled, manipulative, and chillingly plausible portrait of a narcissist fully aware of his own performative power. You always recognise Crowe, but the performance never feels lazy.

The supporting cast is strong throughout, from Michael Shannon's severe Judge Jackson to Leo Wood's grounded Sergeant Triest, with a particularly effective late appearance by Richard E. Grant as Sir Maxwell-Fyfe.

Old-fashioned in the best sense, Nuremberg is a solid, well-crafted production with a disciplined script and minimal - if unavoidable - sentimentality. It doesn't reinvent the genre, but it earns its place within it.

raw_payload.author: gail459
raw_payload.content:
There are very few times in cinematic history that you see a film meld history, dramatic performances, cinematography, editing, and Directing in one movie.

The script is concise. The plot shows the angst of ego, and arrogance. The story lends itself to the present times, and the danger we all need to be in touch with.

Spellbinding!

It must be seen to recognize current evidence of history that is taking place now.

raw_payload.author: pranayjalvi
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg has it's flaws, even though it is marked with a great performance by Russell Crowe. The tragedies and aftermath of war have become a common theme, and is undoubtedly important to address as well.

While the performances were great and it captures the enormity of the trials. But I still feel the story touched the surface of something that was far more complex. The emotional impact during the trials could have been more, the black & white reel style of the court was genuinely lacking.

The film is mostly viewed from Douglas' (Rami Malek) point of view, his connection and analysis of the people who did the most heinous crimes and the roots of it. The conversations between the characters in the film was genuine and moral at times.

Technically, the film is good, if not great. The score is not that important to the story, but still fits with the scenes. No concerns with the cinematography as well. The supporting cast weren't as impactful, and I wish the german cast had actual german actors to make the conversations more authentic.

Overall, Nuremberg is far from being the best docudrama film on atrocities of war, however Crowe's standout performance will make you forget other aspects, but not his presence.

My Rating : 3.5/5.

raw_payload.author: jasonwilkerson-87235
raw_payload.content: Nuremberg was a remarkable film featuring exceptional performances from a talented cast, showcasing their impressive range. While the movie generally met expectations, it's important to note that personal interest in the subject matter is crucial for enjoyment. As an expert in both the Civil War and World War II, I found several inconsistencies that didn't align with historical facts, especially for those familiar with the trial. Despite these discrepancies, Russell Crowe delivered a standout performance, consistently bringing something unique to the screen. Overall, it's an engaging film, though it does contain notable inaccuracies or perhaps depicts events in a way that diverges from historical accounts.

raw_payload.author: jordiejam
raw_payload.content:
Scary how many of the justifications the Nazi's gave for what they did can still be heard from some prominent political parties today.

I thought it was a thought provoking and well acted movie. I thought some of the artistic license taken to fulfil traditional movie tropes took away from the story in the end.

raw_payload.author: RichardMcCarty
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg is a big disappointment. It is often unfocused, glib, melodramatic and tone deaf.

The film does feature some fine acting. Bravo to Russell Crowe for his awards-worthy performance. Rami Malek lobbied hard for his role and plays it fairly well despite his too frequent bug-eyed smirks. John Slattery essentially revisits Roger Sterling and triumphs most when he utters "Welcome to Nuremberg!" with a straight face. And the always great Michael Shannon finds himself in yet another 2025 historical drama that constantly makes the viewer think "this never happened."

While just one scriptwriter is credited, Nuremberg feels like numerous writers divvied up the work without consulting one another and without any unified understanding whatsoever of the theme. The result is a series of incongruent scenes slapped together in editing that are often transitionally weak. What starts as a fascinating angle to the Nuremberg story is fast diffused by its wandering focus, adding storylines and back stories for the sake of padding and not really advancing the story at all. Dialogue is at times unrealistic and irreverent, creating comic moments unintentionally. And yeah, "that never happened."

This is, of course, an important and fascinating story that deserved a far higher production quality than this mess delivers. Abracadabra indeed.

raw_payload.author: xxxxxdarkmoon
raw_payload.content:
First, I would like to say that this film concerns everyone.

Crimes against human rights concern everyone.

What makes Nuremberg so incredibly captivating is the story based on true events, foremost the most terrifying murders of human beings, in numbers, 6 million human beings have been murdered in an unimaginable, gruesome way. That alone shows how important this movie is, and it shows the people who were responsible for the holocaust, the people who remained. Hitler and Goebbels were the leaders of that crime, but they committed suicide as soon as it was clear the war was lost. Nuremberg is about the remaining Nazi figures, that have to take responsibility for the committed war crimes. Foremost the leading Nazi figures that remained. But everyone who was involved in committing these crimes had to take responsibility. Not only the leaders. The defending strategy, that these people just followed orders is worthless. The following generations all over the world should learn from that. It doesn't matter, if someone just followed orders, to kill innocent human beings. It is a crime against human rights if someone followed these illegal orders.

What also stands out are the perfectly chosen actors. Russell Crowe is, without exaggeration, perhaps the best actor of our time. No one could portray Göring better than Crowe. A charismatic, highly intelligent, manipulative, charming narcissist who considered himself infallible. And he almost succeeded in doing so in the unique, critical Nuremberg Tribunal.

The tension is massive. Also, when it was decided that the execution would take place at midnight, after the verdict, without informing the condemned. The following scenes felt like a horror movie.

In addition to Russell Crowe, this film also made me a fan of Rami Malek. This role could not have been portrayed better. The same goes for Michael Shannon. He was perfect in that role as the prosecute of the world, responsible for condemning those responsible. Shannon did not come across as arrogant, but rather concerned, thoughtful, reflective, and wise, fully aware of his enormous responsibility while the whole world watched and listened. He made it clear why the Allies should not simply execute the leading Nazi figures. That would have made them martyrs. So said Shannon. (Jackson). He made it clear that the Germans had already been humiliated twice and that each time it made them stronger as soon as a beacon of hope like Hitler ignited their anger. And who knows if the Allies could withstand the Germans a third time, strengthened by renewed humiliation. For this reason, a proper court should decide whether the Nazi leaders were guilty or not, so as not to turn the executed nazi leaders into Martyrs.

raw_payload.author: DanTheMan2150AD
raw_payload.content: While at times it can be a bit too Americanised for its own good, Nuremberg finds its power in quiet confrontation rather than spectacle. The finer and more complex details are glossed over or often overly summarised in order for its mechanical construction to work; yet when the real focus is on the dialogue and commanding performances, using quiet courtroom tension to explore guilt, justice and the limits of law in the face of unprecedented crimes, the film's ambition becomes clear. It's a fascinating ethical quandary, with the tête-a-tête between Göring and Kelley delivering an enthralling moral dilemma in its reminder that the people who committed these atrocities were human beings. Though the film has a surprising sense of humour, including several amusing smash-cuts, director James Vanderbilt is careful to give the weighty material the respect and space it deserves. A reflection on how hatred disguises itself as ideology that's well-paced, expertly performed and serving as an urgent call to stand up to fascism, Nuremberg is a powerful, sweeping story of the attempt to bring an unthinkable evil to justice.

raw_payload.author: g-s01
raw_payload.content: The story on the whole was quite good, climax wasn't quite as good, even the court proceedings lacked the hype that was created during the whole movie. I was moved by the acting of everyone nonetheless. The characters of family of Hermann Goering, and American-Jew soldier could have been explored more. All in all, a historical anecdote which one should know about.

raw_payload.author: bnfdhxgtn
raw_payload.content:
Although the movie was 2 1/2 hours long, it only depicted one day in court.

I watched two documentaries on the Nuremberg trials before I went to see the movie for no other reason then to try and grasp what would be exaggerated and or tweaked for today's audience. I found that it was historically close to accurate according to what we know, but falling short of the total event. This is just a snapshot. It left me wanting more.

Maybe should've been a series on HBO 10 episode episodes.

This movie depicted parallels to what's going on in the world today and how people are overlooking the signs. (We let it happen) Nazism, fascism, narcissistic leadership, Authoritarian playbooks lead to dictatorial regimes.

Enlightened statesman will not always be at the Helm.

raw_payload.author: bankofmarquis
raw_payload.content:
The 2025 film NUREMBERG proves 2 things...

1). Russell Crowe, when he is interested and invested, can still pack one wallop of a punch as a performer.

2). A strong central performance - in and of itself - cannot elevate a film with a weaker script and dodgey directing.

And...that is what we get in this James Vanderbilt (TRUTH) Directed and Written adaption of the 2013 book THE NAZI AND THE PSYCHIATRIST which depicts the relationship between 2nd in Command of the German/Nazi/Hitler regime, Herman Goring and the Psychiatrist, Douglas Kelley who was brought in to get to know/understand him prior to his trial for War Crimes in Nuremberg, Germany in 1945/1946.

As stated above, Crowe commands the screen as Goring and it is a strong, Oscar-caliber performance by a former A list star that has faded in the past few years. It's good to see this star dust off his star-like qualities so they can shine once again on the silver screen.

Unfortunately, his counterpart, Rami Malek (playing Psychiatrist Douglas Kelley) does not fare as well, resorting to tricks and ticks to make his character seem somewhat interesting when trying to play against Crowe. It was a characterization that got more and more annoying as the film went on.

And that is too bad for Director/Writer Vanderbilt spends the first 1/2 of this film with, basically, just these 2 characters getting to know each other. It takes about 1 hour 15 minutes (of the 2 hour 28 minute runtime) to get to the trial.

And, by that time, you sit up in your seat and look forward to the fireworks of the Courtroom drama...and those fireworks just never quite come.

Michael Shannon (James Garfield in the recent DEATH BY LIGHTNING) comes on strong in the first bit of the film as Justice Robert H. Jackson, the person who insisted that Goring be put on trial (rather than just being summarily shot). He shines in his early scenes, but when his character - as the leading prosecutor in the trial - is supposed to take center stage, he (or rather, his character) just doesn't. It's a waste of talent/potential, squandered by Vanderbilt.

John Slattery, Leo Woodall (who, one might argue, has the best monologue in the film) and (especially) the always good Richard E. Grant all shine in the limited roles that they are given...it's too bad that they didn't have more to do.

Nuremberg is a film with a strong premise, good actors and an interesting courtroom drama that just doesn't quite live up to it's potential - and the end just doesn't pack the punch that it needs to.

Thank goodness Crowe is bringing his "A" game to elevate things.

Letter Grade: B

7 stars (out of 10) and you can take that to the BankofMarquis.

raw_payload.author: marcwhite-33552
raw_payload.content: One of the best and scariest movies I've seen. Kelly's observation that these guys were not nuts, but regular run-of-mill narcissists is spot on. Unfortunately, the goals of the Nuremburg trials, i.e, the end of criminal barbarism in the world, has not materialized. In some countries it's making a resurgence. The similarities to many in power is unmistakable.

raw_payload.author: stevegriffin-93522
raw_payload.content:
A good film but don't expect too much. The storyline was well done via the link to the psychiatrist attached to the prisoners.

Russell Crowe was outstanding; Michael Shannon good; Rami Malek unfortunately was barely believable - I felt he was a miscast, but the film overall got past this.

The film goes on a bit too long and there is a bit of padding. I refer here to the Goering family being pushed in as part of the story. I didn't see the point of that.

raw_payload.author: gabs-55210
raw_payload.content:
I have seen many films of a similar nature and learnt more with each one. Unlike others i have seen this better explains the potential explainations and defence intended by the main defendant played by russel crowe, and enlightened me as to the duplicity and unfairness of the actual trial by other countries, including my own, which i found both informative and worrying that i am only now learning of it.

A thought provoking and enjoyable to watch film based on the trial at Nuremburgh, and as such i rated 8/10.

raw_payload.author: Stargrazingreviews
raw_payload.content: Nuremberg is an overly Americanised and dramatic account that is more entertaining than expected before a significant tonal shift in the 3rd act. Crowe is surprisingly very good as narcissist Goring and is the dramatic core of the film, but Rami Malek's smarmy psychologist is not a very good foil for him. The tone of the first half is dumber and less restrained than expected (Gentlemen, welcome to Nuremberg!', really?). However, the real footage of the camps in the third act is harrowing and ultimately gives the ending the depth the subject matter deserved.

raw_payload.author: jfdvet
raw_payload.content:
After viewing this feature, my feelings about it are mixed. As a lifelong military history scholar, I found the picture to be somewhat stagey and over dramatized when compared to other depictions of the Nuremberg trials.

Its best moments are when an Army psychologist interviews Goring and he gives some thought provoking challenges to the Doctor's accusations. The Nazi leader is depicted as friendly, charming, but also arrogant and self righteous with an underlying feeling of menace. The picture's art direction is also good, though there are a few inaccuracies, which is to be expected from a Hollywood dramatic work.

However, the film too much encapsulates the trial and the events leading up to it. The attention of the story is placed on Goring and mentions the other defendants only in passing. Many important facts are not brought up, especially the involvement and strong influence of the Soviet delegation.

And while he makes an effort, a bloated Russel Crowe gives only a fair performance as Goring. He lacks the sharp intensity and harshness of the real Reichsmarshall.

There also seems to be an implied message of the situation in Germany being reflective of our own turbulent times, which I find to be grossly inaccurate and overtly political. Granted, while America could be in danger of becoming a dictatorial state, our present situation is nowhere near that of Weimar Germany.

Overall OK, but it has nothing like the power and message of Stanley Kramer's masterpiece "Judgement at Nuremberg".

raw_payload.author: jdavidflowers
raw_payload.content:
Great acting across the board with some really big names in the cast. Beautifully filmed with great sets, costumes, music, and backdrops. They did a great job of catching the importance of this historic event.

It got really suspenseful in the end, especially if you didn't remember how things went down from your history class. Excellent work. Something that is few and far between these days in our movies and show.

raw_payload.author: yashbee
raw_payload.content:
The movie is a stark, disciplined, and often unsettling historical drama that succeeds not by excess, but by precision. It understands that the true horror of its subject does not require spectacle alone. It requires restraint, moral tension, and moments of carefully deployed shock.

The standout achievement is unquestionably Rami Malek's performance, which is nothing short of astonishing. Malek brings a deeply controlled intensity to his role, avoiding caricature or melodrama entirely. His acting operates in the micro-expressions: a pause held half a second too long, a gaze that flickers between defiance and dread, a voice that never quite settles into certainty. This restraint makes his performance all the more powerful. Rather than demanding attention, he commands it quietly, pulling the audience into the psychological weight of the proceedings. It is the kind of performance that lingers after the credits roll, not because it overwhelms, but because it refuses to let the viewer disengage.

The pacing of the film is another major strength. For a courtroom-centered historical drama, Nuremberg moves with surprising momentum. The film never feels rushed, yet it never stagnates. Each scene feels purposeful, building logically and emotionally upon the last. The narrative progression mirrors the unfolding moral gravity of the trials themselves, gradually tightening the pressure rather than front-loading its intensity. This careful rhythm allows the audience time to absorb the arguments, the silences, and the implications of what is being judged.

raw_payload.author: Paulmullane46
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg (2025) is an intense historical drama that focuses less on the battlefield and more on the psychological games played behind bars. Based on the true story of the 1945 trials, the film follows an American psychiatrist, Douglas Kelley (Rami Malek), who is sent to interview the captured leaders of the Nazi regime to see if they are mentally fit for trial.

The heart of the movie is the "cat-and-mouse" relationship between Kelley and Hermann Göring, played by Russell Crowe. Crowe is the standout here; he portrays Göring not as a simple villain, but as a charming, clever, and manipulative man who tries to win over his captors. Watching Crowe and Malek face off in a small prison cell is where the film is at its best.

In layman's terms, it's a high-stakes legal thriller about whether you can truly understand "evil" by talking to it. While the courtroom scenes in the second half are solid, some viewers might find the film a bit slow or "stuffy" because it relies heavily on long conversations. Additionally, while Rami Malek is good, some critics felt his performance was a bit too twitchy compared to the more grounded actors around him, like Michael Shannon.

Despite a few slow moments, the film succeeds because it makes a famous historical event feel personal and urgent. It serves as a chilling reminder of how dangerous a charismatic leader can be, even when they've lost everything. It is a smart, well-acted movie that is definitely worth a watch if you enjoy history or psychological dramas.

raw_payload.author: GB65
raw_payload.content: I thoroughly enjoyed this representation of one of the most unusual but important 'military tribunals' in memory. We all know the general story and outcomes from the trials but this concentrated on the relationship between the ultimately tragic US Army psychiatrist, Major Dr Douglas Kelley, and one of the masterminds of the 'Final Solution.' Crowe produces one of his finest performances in years in playing Herman Göring, Hitler's No2. At times you can feel why Kelley seemed to almost fall for his charms and help him on occasion. On the other hand, I felt Rami Malek was completely miscast in this role of Kelley. His facial expressions (sometimes with his permanent smirk on display) completely undermined his acting. As one UK film critic wrote, the problem with him was he spent the whole film looking like Freddie Mercury. A mention too for young British actor Leo Woodall as Howie, assigned as Kelley's translator.

raw_payload.author: MindfulMovieBuff
raw_payload.content:
Nuremberg left me disappointed, despite being a genre and subject I usually care deeply about. The main problem lies with the screenplay, and only then with the direction. The story feels flat and underdeveloped.

Russell Crowe, however, is a clear exception. His portrayal of Goering is convincing, charismatic and narcissistic. He brings complexity and presence to the role, and there's little to fault in his performance.

Unfortunately, the film around him doesn't rise to the same level. The psychological examination aspect is barely explored, and the dramatic tension of the trial (the moral, intellectual, and ideological clashes) is largely missing. What could have been a layered exploration of responsibility, madness, and justice ends up feeling shallow and flat.

raw_payload.author: dtucker86
raw_payload.content: I have always loved history especially about World War II and this is the best movie I have seen about the infamous Nuremberg trials in which Hitler's top henchmen faced the bar of justice. Russell Crowe plays Herman Goring the top Nazi captured alive and Rami Malek is the Army psychiatrist who forms a disturbing bond with him while trying to understand the true nature of evil (he sure picked a good group of men to study!). Malek overacts at times and his character comes across as unprofessional and downright weird! But Crowe more then makes up for that with his masterful portrayal of Goring. The only major quibble I have with the movie is that for some reason it seems to want to cast Goring in a somewhat sympathetic light!

raw_payload.author: dhayob-94911
raw_payload.content:
If ChatGPT were asked to write a script about the subject matter you'd get something similar. Predictable beats and does not nearly capture the gravity of the occasion portrayed. Good acting does not make up for a desperate desire of the script to make an impact through dialogue more befitting a made for TV movie.

Entertaining but this story deserved better.

raw_payload.author: Filip16
raw_payload.content: Great, disturbing and right on time with the world at breakpoint of descending into a battlefield once more. Dark times are ahead, and the psyche of hate hides in them, but also in us. Göring and Kelly were, for a brief moment, true friends, reminding us that hope is real, but fragile. We must keep it safe, because it will be tested to unforeseen extents. We are all human is all I can say for now.

raw_payload.author: julieshotmail
raw_payload.content:
This thing has prestige movie written all over it, with heavy hitters like Russell Crowe and Rami Malek leading the charge and instantly raising expectations. Then you add Michael Shannon and Colin Hanks into the mix and suddenly the cast alone feels like a courtroom of acting royalty, which fits perfectly since the story drops us into one of the darkest chapters in world history. The narrative mostly grabs you by the collar and doesn't let go, equal parts gripping and educational, and Crowe in particular disappears into the role of Goring so completely that you almost forget you are watching an actor at work.

There are moments, though, when the dialogue makes you pause, when you find yourself wondering if people really talked like that back then or if Hollywood took a few creative liberties, but even those scenes don't derail the overall experience. The real surprise for me comes from Leo Woodall, the British actor I only knew as the sketchy nephew causing trouble in Italy on "The White Lotus," who shows up here as an American soldier doubling as a German translator. At first you just assume he is bilingual and move on, but then you learn he didn't know a word of German before landing this role and actually went out and learned the language just for the movie, which somehow makes his performance even more impressive. That kind of commitment deserves a slow clap at the very least.
